Artist Official Representation

ROBINEAU is pleased to announce the worldwide representation of Vowa Mank.

Born in 1985 in Yakutia, Mank studied photography in Milan and built a career across fashion and design before becoming a full-time artist. His paintings are constructed through thousands of dots, like pixels, in a meticulous accumulation of micro-elements.

In his Twelve Apostles series, this point-by-point technique becomes a reflection of our digital lives and a search for groundedness in a changing world. Seen up close and from a distance, the image shifts between abstraction and legibility, drawing the viewer closer to figures that feel both classical and contemporary.

Artwork enquiry

INFRA / ULTRA

Exhibition Now Opened

ROBINEAU Gallery is pleased to announce INFRA / ULTRA, open to the public until Saturday, 1 August 2026.

Featuring Nina Sechko, Kebeyo Chen, and marking the official representation of Vowa Mank, the exhibition explores the threshold between the visible and the unseen.

Drawing on Maurice Merleau-Ponty and Wassily Kandinsky, the exhibition examines how form, texture, rhythm and material presence can reveal what escapes immediate sight, yet continues to shape perception.

View the catalogue

Podsosensky Pereulok 20c4, Moscow
Thursday to Saturday, 12:00 to 20:00

Collector Intelligence

We have launched a new section on our website: Collector Intelligence.

This section is dedicated to helping collectors better understand contemporary art, market context, artist positioning and the decisions that shape meaningful collections.

The first article focuses on Zhou Song, represented in Russia by ROBINEAU Gallery. It looks at his museum exhibitions, international recognition, market position, and why collectors are paying attention now.

Read Collector Intelligence

Nina Sechko at Kiaf Seoul

Solo Presentation

ROBINEAU Gallery is proud to announce its participation in KIAF Seoul, where the gallery will present an exclusive solo exhibition by Nina Sechko.

Titled The Room of Light Preservation, the project draws upon Daoist philosophy and its resonance throughout East Asian thought. The works invite viewers into a contemplative space where light becomes both a spiritual metaphor and a living force.

At the centre of the presentation is a sculptural composition in which two figures support a box raised above their heads. Within this elevated enclosure rests a third figure, a girl holding a softly glowing pearl.
